description: Cross-paper edges of the NSE blowup / non-uniqueness proof dependency graph. Each edge connects theorem-level node IDs across papers. Direction reads subject-verb-object: '{from} {type} {to}'.

edges.note: Tao re-derives Cheskidov's blowup for $\alpha < 1/3$ via truncated dyadic model with pump gates. Cheskidov uses Sobolev-inversion; Tao uses explicit mode-by-mode construction with exogenous time cutoffs.
BMR prove Tao's 2009 conjecture: regularity holds under $\intds/sg^2=\infty$. TRIANGLE: Tao-averaged:rem:$5.2 \to$ BMR:thm:$1.1 \to$ Tao-hyper:sec:1.
Proof direction $(1.1\Rightarrow1.2)$: Corollary 11.1 (bounded enstrophy) promotes smooth finite-energy solution to $H^1$ solution.
Corollary 4.3 (almost smooth $H^1$ solutions are essentially mild) gives the integral equation (1.5).
Theorem 5.4 parts (ii), (iii), (iv): $H^1$ local well-posedness yields $H^k$ regularity for all k; uniqueness of mild solutions; smoothness of u and p.
Waleffe reduces K-P model to dyadic Burgers (eq:17). Tao's prop:5.1 uses a truncated version of the same dyadic chain with viscous dissipation and explicit pump-gate scheduling.
Waleffe proves $H^\alpha$ blowup for inviscid dyadic models (any $\alpha > 0)$. Tao extends to viscous case $(\alpha < 1/2)$ using pump gates with exogenous time truncation.
Cheskidov sec:2: 'As it was proposed in [W] in the case $\nu=0$, we simplify the model.' Adopts Waleffe's chain reduction of the K-P model, extends to viscous case.
Palasek's Leray-Hopf definition for the Obukhov dyadic model directly extends Cheskidov's dyadic Leray-Hopf class.
Prop 3.7 constructs two non-negative solutions of the Obukhov model. Cheskidov's thm:4.2 proves non-negativity preservation and energy inequality upgrades.
Palasek's non-uniqueness operates in the same supercritical dyadic regime as Cheskidov's blowup.
Rem 1.3 states 'The lower bound $\alpha > 2$ is sharp (subcritical regime has uniqueness).' BMR's thm:1.1 provides this uniqueness.
BMR's regularity threshold is tight: non-uniqueness begins exactly where BMR's dissipation condition fails.
Tao's pump-gate cascade is the structural predecessor of Palasek's non-uniqueness mechanism.
Palasek's Prop 2.1 is a quantitative strengthening of Cheskidov's Leray-Hopf existence.
rem:1.5 cites Tao's averaged NSE blowup as the foundational program underpinning the non-uniqueness strategy.
rem:1.5 references the challenge of embedding Obukhov dyadic model behavior into actual NSE.
rem:1.5 explicitly states the construction uses 'a simplified version of the mechanism proposed by the second author in [palasek2024non]'.
The two principal parts $v^{(1)}/v^{(2)}$ are the simplified analog of Palasek's two stable-manifold branches.
prop:4.3 is the NSE analog of Palasek's prop:3.7 bootstrap construction.
rem:1.4 explicitly cites Hou's regularity theorem as showing $BMO^{-1}$ is the natural well-posedness threshold.
Individual solutions are regular (Hou), but the Cauchy problem is not well-posed — two distinct regular solutions can share the same critical data.
Companion results by same authors using the same technical framework.
Identical geometric lemma from Beekie-Buckmaster-Vicol.
Same $L^p$ localization/decoupling lemma from Cheskidov-Modena-Szekely.
Same Besov-space maximal regularity theorem for the heat equation.
Identical definition of Chemin-Lerner mixed time-space Besov norms.
Both construct the key $R^3-specific$ correction via forced linearized NSE.
2602.19074v1 extends 2412.10404's $R^3$ convex integration scheme to the 2D torus $T^2$.
2602.19074v1's perturbation flow is the $T^2$ analogue of 2412.10404's non-stationary correction.
Parallel non-uniqueness results with fundamentally different methods.
Parallel non-uniqueness results in different regularity classes.
Parallel Leray-Hopf non-uniqueness results using wholly different techniques.
prop:4.2 is the vorticity conservation law with explicit attribution to 2402.13229v1.
Both extract nontrivial ancient Euler solutions from Type II blowup sequences.
Both exclude Type II blowup at the origin under integrability conditions.
The Type I blowup solution satisfies Hou's weak-* continuity in $BMO^{-1}$.
prop:6.2 verifies the constructed solution lies in the Koch-Tataru path space.
Together these sharply pin the Ladyzhenskaya-Prodi-Serrin threshold at p = 2.
The incompressible perturbation technique developed in 2412.09637 directly precedes the $T^2$ result.
Both prove non-uniqueness at critical regularity thresholds via distinct mechanisms.
Tao's averaged NSE blowup motivates the broader program of proving pathological behavior in NS-like equations.
BMNV replaces rank-2 Beltrami projectors with rank-1 pipe flow projectors.
BMNV replaces Beltrami waves with intermittent pipe flows.
BMNV's iterated inverse divergence achieves $(\lambda/\zeta)^d$ polynomial gain.
Both are $L^p$ decorrelation lemmas for slow $\times$ fast products.
BMNV introduces two nested inductions for $H^{1/2-}$ regularity.
BMNV proves flexibility for 3D Euler in $C^0_t H^{1/2-}_x$ — the sharp Onsager threshold.
2412.10404's inverse divergence iteration step is directly imported from BMNV.

Buckmaster-Vicol's thm:1.2 (non-uniqueness of NSE weak solutions in $C^0_t H^\beta_x)$ extends Isett's Euler construction to the Navier-Stokes equations. The convex integration scheme is adapted from Euler to NSE with viscous dissipation absorbing errors.

BV's main iterative proposition adapts Isett's Main Lemma (lem:2.1) structure: frequency-energy iteration $(\Xi,e_v,e_R) \to (\Xi',e_v',e_R')$ with N-dependent gains. The key modification is adapting the stress correction to handle NSE viscous terms.

BMNV's $H^{1/2-}$ Euler non-uniqueness extends Isett's $C^\alpha_{t,x}$ result, reaching the sharp $L^2-based$ Onsager threshold. The pipe flow construction and higher-order stresses are the key advances beyond Isett's Mikado-type flows.

BMNV's iterated inverse divergence (prop:A.17) extends Isett's parametrix construction (prop:17.6). Isett uses Fourier series expansion; BMNV iterates d times for polynomial gain $(\lambda/\zeta)^d$ while preserving compact support.

BV adopts Isett's Euler-Reynolds framework (def:2.1): $\partial_t$ v $+ 
abla\cdot(v$⊗$v) + 
ablap = 
abla\cdotR$. The symmetric stress tensor R captures the Reynolds stress error in convex integration.
BV adopts Isett's frequency-energy level framework (def:2.2): bounds ‖$\nabla^k$ v‖ $\le \Xi^k e_v^{1/2}$, ‖$\nabla^k$ R‖ $\le \Xi^k e_R$. This parameterization governs the iteration.
BMNV's spatial disjointness of pipe flows (prop:4.8) replaces Isett's temporal gluing approximation (lem:3.2). Isett localizes stress in time; BMNV achieves disjointness in space via pipe geometry.

Miao-Nie-Ye's $R^3$ non-uniqueness (thm:1.2) is a descendant of Isett's Onsager construction. The convex integration framework — Euler-Reynolds flows, frequency-energy iteration, Mikado-type perturbations — originates in Isett's proof.

Miao-Nie-Ye's $T^2$ smooth non-uniqueness (thm:1.1) is a descendant of Isett's framework. The 2D result requires different technical tools but the conceptual architecture (convex integration with localized stress corrections) traces to Isett.

Proof of prop:2.11 explicitly cites 'Gronwall inequality [Lemma B.3]' from Coiculescu-Palasek (2503.14699) — the singular Gronwall inequality with (t-s)^{-1/2} kernel used to close the bootstrap argument for w = u - v.
CL's main iteration proposition uses the NSE-Reynolds framework from BV (prop:2.1), adding temporal concentration as the new mechanism on top of BV's convex integration scheme.
BV (thm:1.2) proved nonuniqueness in $H^{-1+\beta}$ below the critical Sobolev threshold; CL sharpens this to $L^p_t L^\infty (p < 2)$, which is the sharp Ladyzhenskaya-Prodi-Serrin endpoint.
The NSE-Reynolds system formulation (well-prepared solutions, stress tensor, relaxation) is adopted from BV's framework (prop:2.1 and surrounding definitions).
CL's sharp gluing (prop:3.2) — splicing together short-time smooth NSE solutions to concentrate the stress — directly adapts Isett's gluing approximation (lem:3.2) for the viscous setting.
CL's anomalous dissipation result (thm:1.11, solutions in $L^{3/2-eps}_t C^{1/3})$ places their solutions exactly at the Onsager threshold whose negative direction Isett proved (thm:1 of 1608.08301).

Luo2025 proves strong ill-posedness (norm inflation) in Ḃˢₚ,q supercritical spaces; CL2022 (same author Luo) proves non-uniqueness of weak solutions in $L^p_t L^\infty$ below LPS threshold. Both papers establish the borderline nature of NSE well-posedness in the same supercritical regime.

Luo2025 (norm inflation via oscillatory construction) and BV2019 (non-uniqueness via convex integration) both establish ill-behavior of NSE below critical Sobolev regularity, using fundamentally different mechanisms.

Both 2025 papers study fine-scale behavior at the regularity threshold: CP2025 constructs non-unique smooth solutions from critical data, Luo2025 shows sharp norm inflation for small data in supercritical Besov spaces. Cited together in the same cluster of results.

Both prove non-uniqueness of Leray-class NSE solutions; BV2019 uses convex integration on $T^3 (no$ forcing), ABC uses Vishik's unstable eigenvalue on $\mathbb{R}^3 (with$ forcing). Fundamentally different mechanisms producing the same type of non-uniqueness.

ABC2022 and CL2022 both establish Leray-class non-uniqueness. CL uses convex integration to achieve non-uniqueness in $L^p_t L^\infty (sharp$ LPS threshold), ABC uses Vishik instability for Leray-Hopf with forcing. Cited together as the two main approaches.

Yang et al (Hou+DeepMind, Sep 2025) extend ABC's result to the UNFORCED setting: both papers prove non-uniqueness of Leray-Hopf solutions, but Yang et al remove the need for a body force using a computer-assisted unstable eigenpair computation. ABC is the direct predecessor.

Cheskidov-Dai-Palasek (CDP2025) prove non-uniqueness of Leray solutions via Type I blow-up + non-uniqueness for any smooth data — a different mechanism from ABC's Vishik instability approach. Both are Leray non-uniqueness results but with distinct techniques.
Miao-Nie-Ye (2025) prove non-uniqueness of smooth NSE solutions on torus, extending the ABC program to a different setting (smooth solutions, no force, on $T^3)$.

BCV2021 extends BV2019's non-uniqueness result: BV2019 proves non-unique weak NSE solutions with singular time set $dim(\Sigma_T)=1$; BCV2021 additionally glues between any two strong solutions and achieves $dim(\Sigma_T) < 1$. Buckmaster and Vicol are co-authors on both.

BCV2021 uses the intermittent convex integration scheme (developed for Euler in Isett2018) adapted to NSE with temporal intermittency. The Beltrami wave structure and iteration scheme builds on Isett's framework.

ABC2022 (Albritton-Brué-Colombo) and BCV2021 both prove non-uniqueness of weak NSE solutions, but by different methods: BCV uses convex integration to glue strong solutions; ABC uses Vishik's unstable eigenvalue to construct Leray-Hopf solutions. Colombo is co-author on both.
